subject: Chemistry
question_text: Express the change in internal energy of a system when No heat is absorbed by the system from the surroundings, but work (w) is done on the system. What type of wall does the system have? No work is done on the system, but q amount of heat is taken out from the system and given to the surroundings. What type of wall does the system have? w amount of work is done by the system and q amount of heat is supplied to the system. What type of system would it be?
question_html:
Express the change in internal energy of a system when

  1. No heat is absorbed by the system from the surroundings, but work (w) is done on the system. What type of wall does the system have?
  2. No work is done on the system, but q amount of heat is taken out from the system and given to the surroundings. What type of wall does the system have?
  3. w amount of work is done by the system and q amount of heat is supplied to the system. What type of system would it be?
solution_text: {tex}\Delta{/tex} U = w ad , wall is adiabatic {tex}\Delta{/tex} U = – q, thermally conducting walls {tex}\Delta{/tex} U = q – w, closed system.
solution_html:
1. {tex}\Delta{/tex} U = w ad, wall is adiabatic
  2. {tex}\Delta{/tex} U = – q, thermally conducting walls
  3. {tex}\Delta{/tex} U = q – w, closed system.

question_text: If water vapour is assumed to be a perfect gas, the molar enthalpy change for vapourization of 1 mol of water at 1bar and 100°C is 41kJ mol –1 . Calculate the internal energy change, when 1 mol of water is vapourised at 1 bar pressure and 100°C.
question_html: If water vapour is assumed to be a perfect gas, the molar enthalpy change for vapourization of 1 mol of water at 1bar and 100°C is 41kJ mol–1. Calculate the internal energy change, when 1 mol of water is vapourised at 1 bar pressure and 100°C.
solution_text: The change = H 2 O (l) {tex}\longrightarrow{/tex} H 2 O (g) {tex}\Delta{/tex} H = {tex}\Delta{/tex} U + {tex}\Delta{/tex} n g RT or {tex}\Delta{/tex} U = {tex}\Delta{/tex} H - {tex}\Delta{/tex} n g RT, substituting the values, we get {tex}\Delta{/tex} U = 41.00 kJ mol -1 - 1 {tex}\times{/tex} 8.3 J mol -1 K -1 {tex}\times{/tex} 373 K = 41.00 kJ mol -1 – 3.096 kJ mol -1 = 37.904 kJ mol –1
solution_html:
The change = H2O (l) {tex}\longrightarrow{/tex} H2O (g)
{tex}\Delta{/tex}H = {tex}\Delta{/tex}U + {tex}\Delta{/tex}ngRT
or {tex}\Delta{/tex}U = {tex}\Delta{/tex}H - {tex}\Delta{/tex}ngRT, substituting the values, we get
{tex}\Delta{/tex}U = 41.00 kJ mol-1 - 1 {tex}\times{/tex} 8.3 J mol-1 K-1 {tex}\times{/tex} 373 K
= 41.00 kJ mol-1 – 3.096 kJ mol-1

question_text: 1g of graphite is burnt in a bomb calorimeter in excess of oxygen at 298 K and 1 atmospheric pressure according to the equation C (graphite) + O 2 (g) {tex}\longrightarrow{/tex} CO 2 (g) During the reaction, the temperature rises from 298 K to 299 K. If the heat capacity of the bomb calorimeter is 20.7kJ/K, what is the enthalpy change for the above reaction at 298 K and 1 atm?
question_html: 1g of graphite is burnt in a bomb calorimeter in excess of oxygen at 298 K and 1 atmospheric pressure according to the equation C (graphite) + O2 (g) {tex}\longrightarrow{/tex} CO2 (g) During the reaction, the temperature rises from 298 K to 299 K. If the heat capacity of the bomb calorimeter is 20.7kJ/K, what is the enthalpy change for the above reaction at 298 K and 1 atm?
solution_text: Suppose q is the quantity of heat from the reaction mixture and C V is the heat capacity of the calorimeter, then the quantity of heat absorbed by the calorimeter. q = C V {tex}\times{/tex} {tex}\Delta{/tex} T Quantity of heat from the reaction will have the same magnitude but opposite sign because the heat lost by the system (reaction mixture) is equal to the heat gained by the calorimeter. {tex}q=-C_{V} \times \Delta T{/tex} = -20.7 kJ / K {tex}\times{/tex} (299-298) K = - 20.7 kJ (Here, the negative sign indicates the exothermic nature of the reaction) Thus, ∆U for the combustion of the 1g of graphite = – 20.7 kJK –1 For combustion of 1 mol of graphite, = {tex}\frac{12.0 \mathrm{g} \mathrm{mol}^{-1} \times(-20.7 \mathrm{kJ})}{1 \mathrm{g}}{/tex} = {tex}-2.48 \times 10^{2} \mathrm{kJ} \mathrm{mol}^{-1}{/tex} , Since {tex}\Delta{/tex} n g = 0 {tex}\Delta H=\Delta U{/tex} = - 2.48 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 2 kJ mol –1
solution_html:
Suppose q is the quantity of heat from the reaction mixture and CV is the heat capacity of the calorimeter, then the quantity of heat absorbed by the calorimeter.
q = CV {tex}\times{/tex} {tex}\Delta{/tex}T
Quantity of heat from the reaction will have the same magnitude but opposite sign because the heat lost by the system (reaction mixture) is equal to the heat gained by the calorimeter.
{tex}q=-C_{V} \times \Delta T{/tex} = -20.7 kJ / K {tex}\times{/tex} (299-298) K
= - 20.7 kJ
(Here, the negative sign indicates the exothermic nature of the reaction)
Thus, ∆U for the combustion of the 1g of graphite = – 20.7 kJK–1
For combustion of 1 mol of graphite,
= {tex}\frac{12.0 \mathrm{g} \mathrm{mol}^{-1} \times(-20.7 \mathrm{kJ})}{1 \mathrm{g}}{/tex}
= {tex}-2.48 \times 10^{2} \mathrm{kJ} \mathrm{mol}^{-1}{/tex}, Since {tex}\Delta{/tex}ng = 0
{tex}\Delta H=\Delta U{/tex} = - 2.48 {tex}\times{/tex} 102 kJ mol–1

question_text: A swimmer coming out from a pool is covered with a film of water weighing about 18g. How much heat must be supplied to evaporate this water at 298 K? Calculate the internal energy of vaporization at 298K. {tex}\Delta_{\text {vap}} H^{\ominus}{/tex} for water at 298K = 44.01kJ mol –1
question_html:
A swimmer coming out from a pool is covered with a film of water weighing about 18g. How much heat must be supplied to evaporate this water at 298 K? Calculate the internal energy of vaporization at 298K.
{tex}\Delta_{\text {vap}} H^{\ominus}{/tex} for water at 298K = 44.01kJ mol–1
solution_text: We can represent the process of evaporation as {tex}\mathop {{{\text{H}}_2}{\text{O}}(1)}\limits_{1\,{\text{mol}}} \xrightarrow{{{\text{vapertation}}}}\mathop {{{\text{H}}_2}{\text{O}}({\text{g}})}\limits_{1\,{\text{mol}}} {/tex} No. of moles in 18 g H 2 O(l) is = {tex}\frac{18 g}{18 g m o l^{-1}}{/tex} = 1 mol Heat supplied to evaporate18g water at 298 K = n {tex}\times{/tex} {tex}\Delta_{\text {vap}} H^{\ominus}{/tex} = (1 mol) {tex}\times{/tex} (44.01 kJ mol –1 ) = 44.01 kJ (assuming steam behaving as an ideal gas). {tex}\Delta_{\text {uap }} U=\Delta_{\text {uap }} H^{\mathrm{e}}-p \Delta V=\Delta_{\text {oap }} H^{\ominus}-\Delta n_{g} R T{/tex} {tex}\Delta_{\mathrm{eq}} H^{\prime \prime}-\Delta \mathrm{n}_{\mathrm{e}} \mathrm{RT}=44.01 \mathrm{kJ}{/tex} {tex}-(1)\left(8.314 \mathrm{JK}^{-1} \mathrm{mol}^{-1}\right)(298 \mathrm{K})\left(10^{-3} \mathrm{kJJ}^{-1}\right){/tex} {tex}\Delta_{\exp } U^{v}=44.01 \mathrm{kJ}-2.48 \mathrm{kJ}{/tex} {tex}=41.53 \mathrm{kJ}{/tex}
solution_html:
We can represent the process of evaporation as
{tex}\mathop {{{\text{H}}_2}{\text{O}}(1)}\limits_{1\,{\text{mol}}} \xrightarrow{{{\text{vapertation}}}}\mathop {{{\text{H}}_2}{\text{O}}({\text{g}})}\limits_{1\,{\text{mol}}} {/tex}
No. of moles in 18 g H2O(l) is
= {tex}\frac{18 g}{18 g m o l^{-1}}{/tex} = 1 mol
Heat supplied to evaporate18g water at
298 K = n {tex}\times{/tex} {tex}\Delta_{\text {vap}} H^{\ominus}{/tex}
= (1 mol) {tex}\times{/tex} (44.01 kJ mol–1)
= 44.01 kJ
(assuming steam behaving as an ideal gas).
{tex}\Delta_{\text {uap }} U=\Delta_{\text {uap }} H^{\mathrm{e}}-p \Delta V=\Delta_{\text {oap }} H^{\ominus}-\Delta n_{g} R T{/tex}
{tex}\Delta_{\mathrm{eq}} H^{\prime \prime}-\Delta \mathrm{n}_{\mathrm{e}} \mathrm{RT}=44.01 \mathrm{kJ}{/tex} {tex}-(1)\left(8.314 \mathrm{JK}^{-1} \mathrm{mol}^{-1}\right)(298 \mathrm{K})\left(10^{-3} \mathrm{kJJ}^{-1}\right){/tex}
{tex}\Delta_{\exp } U^{v}=44.01 \mathrm{kJ}-2.48 \mathrm{kJ}{/tex}
{tex}=41.53 \mathrm{kJ}{/tex}

question_text: Assuming the water vapor to be a perfect gas, calculate the internal energy change when 1 mol of water at 100°C and 1 bar pressure is converted to the ice at 0°C. Given the enthalpy of fusion of ice is 6.00 kJ mol1 heat capacity of water is 4.2 J/g°C.
question_html: Assuming the water vapor to be a perfect gas, calculate the internal energy change when 1 mol of water at 100°C and 1 bar pressure is converted to the ice at 0°C. Given the enthalpy of fusion of ice is 6.00 kJ mol1 heat capacity of water is 4.2 J/g°C.
solution_text: The change take place as follows: Step - 1: 1 mol H 2 O (1, 100°C) {tex}\longrightarrow{/tex} 1 mol (1, 0°C) Enthalpy change {tex}\Delta{/tex} H 1 Step - 2: 1 mol H 2 O (1, 0°C) {tex}\longrightarrow{/tex} 1 mol H 2 O( S, 0°C) Enthalpy change {tex}\Delta{/tex} H 2 Total enthalpy change will be - {tex}\Delta{/tex} H = {tex}\Delta{/tex} H 1 + {tex}\Delta{/tex} H 2 {tex}\Delta{/tex} H 1 = - (18 {tex}\times{/tex} 4.2 {tex}\times{/tex} 100) J mol -1 = - 7560 J mol -1 = - 7.56 k J mol -1 {tex}\Delta{/tex} H 2 = - 6.00 kJ mol -1 Therefore, {tex}\Delta{/tex} H = - 7.56 kJ mol -1 + (-6.00 kJ mol -1 ) = -13.56 kJ mol -1 There is negligible change in the volume during the change form liquid to solid state. Therefore, p {tex}\Delta{/tex} v = {tex}\Delta{/tex} ng RT = 0 {tex}\Delta{/tex} H = {tex}\Delta{/tex} U = - 13.56kJ mol -1
solution_html:
The change take place as follows:
Step - 1: 1 mol H2O (1, 100°C) {tex}\longrightarrow{/tex} 1 mol (1, 0°C) Enthalpy change {tex}\Delta{/tex}H1
Step - 2:  1 mol H2O (1, 0°C) {tex}\longrightarrow{/tex} 1 mol H2O( S, 0°C) Enthalpy change {tex}\Delta{/tex}H2
Total enthalpy change will be -
{tex}\Delta{/tex}H = {tex}\Delta{/tex}H1 + {tex}\Delta{/tex}H2
{tex}\Delta{/tex}H1 = - (18 {tex}\times{/tex} 4.2 {tex}\times{/tex} 100) J mol-1
= - 7560 J mol-1 = - 7.56 k J mol-1
{tex}\Delta{/tex}H2 = - 6.00 kJ mol-1
Therefore,
{tex}\Delta{/tex}H = - 7.56 kJ mol-1 + (-6.00 kJ mol-1)
= -13.56 kJ mol-1
There is negligible change in the volume during the change form liquid to solid state.
Therefore, p{tex}\Delta{/tex}v = {tex}\Delta{/tex}ng RT = 0
{tex}\Delta{/tex}H = {tex}\Delta{/tex}U = - 13.56kJ mol-1

question_text: The combustion of one mole of benzene takes place at 298 K and 1 atm. After combustion, CO 2 (g) and H 2 O (I) are produced and 3267.0 kJ of heat is liberated. Calculate the standard enthalpy of formation, {tex}\Delta _ { f } H ^ { \ominus }{/tex} of benzene. Standard enthalpies of formation of CO 2 (g) and H 2 O (I) and -393.5 kJ mol -1 and -285.83 kJ mol -1 respectively.
question_html: The combustion of one mole of benzene takes place at 298 K and 1 atm. After combustion, CO2(g) and H2O (I) are produced and 3267.0 kJ of heat is liberated. Calculate the standard enthalpy of formation, {tex}\Delta _ { f } H ^ { \ominus }{/tex} of benzene. Standard enthalpies of formation of CO2 (g) and H2O (I) and -393.5 kJ mol-1 and -285.83 kJ mol-1 respectively.
solution_text: According to the question, Standard enthalpies of formation of CO 2 (g) and H 2 O (I) and - 393.5 kJ mol -1 and - 285.83 kJ mol -1 respectively. The reaction for the formation of benzene is given by 6C (graphite) + 3H 2 (g) {tex}\longrightarrow{/tex} C 6 H 6 (l) ; {tex}\Delta_f{/tex} H o = ? ..(i) The enthalpy of combustion of 1 mole of benzene is C 6 H 6 (l) + {tex}\frac { 15 } { 2 }{/tex} O 2 {tex}\longrightarrow{/tex} 6CO 2 (g) + 3H 2 O(l) ; {tex}\Delta _ { c }{/tex} H o = - 3265 kJ mol -1 ..(ii) The enthalpy of formation of 1 mole of CO 2 (g) is C (graphite) + O 2 (g) {tex}\longrightarrow{/tex} CO 2 (g); {tex}\Delta _ { f }{/tex} H o = - 393.5 kJ mol -1 ..(iii) The enthalpy of formation of 1 mole of H 2 O(l) is H 2 (g) + {tex}\frac { 1 } { 2 }{/tex} O 2 (g) {tex}\longrightarrow{/tex} H 2 O(l); {tex}\Delta _ { f }{/tex} H o = - 285.83 kJ mol -1 ..(iv) On multiplying Eq. (iii) by 6 and Eq. (iv) by 3 we get 6C (graphite) + 6O 2 (g) {tex}\longrightarrow{/tex} 6CO 2 (g); {tex}\Delta _ { f }{/tex} H o = - 2361 kJ mol -1 3H 2 (g) + {tex}\frac { 3 } { 2 }{/tex} O 2 (g) {tex}\longrightarrow{/tex} 3H 2 O (l); {tex}\Delta _ { f }{/tex} H o = - 857.49 kJ mol -1 Summing up the above two equations: 6C (graphite) + 3H 2 (g) + {tex}\frac { 15 } { 2 }{/tex} O 2 (g) {tex}\longrightarrow{/tex} 6CO 2 (g) + 3H 2 O (l) ; {tex}\Delta _ { f }{/tex} H o = - 3218.49 kJ mol -1 ..(v) On reversing Eqs (ii), we get 6CO 2 (g) + 3H 2 O (l) {tex}\longrightarrow{/tex} C 6 H 6 (l) + {tex}\frac { 15 } { 2 }{/tex} O 2 ; {tex}\Delta _ { f }{/tex} H o = - 3218.49 kJ mol -1 ..(vi) Adding Eqs. (v) and (vi), we get 6C (graphite) + 3H 2 (g) {tex}\longrightarrow{/tex} C 6 H 6 (l); {tex}\Delta _ { f }{/tex} H o = - 48.51 kJ mol -1 .. (vii)
solution_html:
According to the question, Standard enthalpies of formation of CO2 (g) and H2O (I) and - 393.5 kJ mol-1 and - 285.83 kJ mol-1 respectively.
The reaction for the formation of benzene is given by
6C (graphite) + 3H2(g) {tex}\longrightarrow{/tex} C6H6(l) ; {tex}\Delta_f{/tex} Ho = ? ..(i)
The enthalpy of combustion of 1 mole of benzene is
C6H6(l) + {tex}\frac { 15 } { 2 }{/tex}O2 {tex}\longrightarrow{/tex} 6CO2(g) + 3H2O(l) ; {tex}\Delta _ { c }{/tex} Ho = - 3265 kJ mol-1 ..(ii)
The enthalpy of formation of 1 mole of CO2 (g) is
C (graphite) + O2(g) {tex}\longrightarrow{/tex} CO2(g); {tex}\Delta _ { f }{/tex} Ho = - 393.5 kJ mol-1 ..(iii)
The enthalpy of formation of  1 mole of H2O(l) is
H2(g) + {tex}\frac { 1 } { 2 }{/tex}O2(g) {tex}\longrightarrow{/tex} H2O(l); {tex}\Delta _ { f }{/tex} Ho = - 285.83 kJ mol-1 ..(iv)
On multiplying Eq. (iii) by 6 and Eq. (iv) by 3 we get
6C (graphite) + 6O2(g) {tex}\longrightarrow{/tex} 6CO2(g); {tex}\Delta _ { f }{/tex} Ho = - 2361 kJ mol-1
3H2(g) + {tex}\frac { 3 } { 2 }{/tex} O2(g) {tex}\longrightarrow{/tex} 3H2O (l); {tex}\Delta _ { f }{/tex} Ho = - 857.49 kJ mol-1
Summing up the above two equations:
6C (graphite) + 3H2(g) + {tex}\frac { 15 } { 2 }{/tex}O2 (g) {tex}\longrightarrow{/tex} 6CO2 (g) + 3H2O (l) ; {tex}\Delta _ { f }{/tex}Ho = - 3218.49 kJ mol-1 ..(v)
On reversing Eqs (ii), we get
6CO2 (g) + 3H2O (l) {tex}\longrightarrow{/tex} C6H6 (l) + {tex}\frac { 15 } { 2 }{/tex} O2; {tex}\Delta _ { f }{/tex} Ho = - 3218.49 kJ mol-1 ..(vi)
Adding Eqs. (v) and (vi), we get
6C (graphite) + 3H2 (g) {tex}\longrightarrow{/tex} C6H6 (l); {tex}\Delta _ { f }{/tex}Ho = - 48.51 kJ mol-1 .. (vii)

subject: Chemistry
question_text: Predict in which of the following, entropy increases/decreases A liquid crystallizes into a solid. The temperature of a crystallize solid is raised from 0 K to 115 K 2NaHCO 3 (s) {tex}\longrightarrow{/tex} Na 2 CO 3 (s) + CO 2 (g) + H 2 O (g) H 2 (g) {tex}\longrightarrow{/tex} 2H (g)
question_html:
Predict in which of the following, entropy increases/decreases

  1. A liquid crystallizes into a solid.
  2. The temperature of a crystallize solid is raised from 0 K to 115 K
  3. 2NaHCO3(s) {tex}\longrightarrow{/tex} Na2CO3 (s) + CO2 (g) + H2O (g)
  4. H2(g) {tex}\longrightarrow{/tex} 2H (g)
solution_text: After freezing, the molecules attain an ordered state and therefore, entropy decreases. At 0K the constituent particles are in static form therefore entropy is minimum. If the temperature is raised to 115 K, these begin to move and oscillate about their equilibrium positions in the lattice and system becomes more disordered, therefore entropy increases. Reactant, NaHCO 3 is solid and it has low entropy. Among products there are one solid and two gases. Therefore, the products represent a condition of higher entropy. Here one molecule gives two atoms i.e., number of particles increases leading to more disordered state. Two moles of H atoms have higher entropy than one mole of dihydrogen molecule.
solution_html:
1. After freezing, the molecules attain an ordered state and therefore, entropy decreases.
  2. At 0K the constituent particles are in static form therefore entropy is minimum. If the temperature is raised to 115 K, these begin to move and oscillate about their equilibrium positions in the lattice and system becomes more disordered, therefore entropy increases.
  3. Reactant, NaHCO3 is solid and it has low entropy. Among products there are one solid and two gases. Therefore, the products represent a condition of higher entropy.
  4. Here one molecule gives two atoms i.e., number of particles increases leading to more disordered state. Two moles of H atoms have higher entropy than one mole of dihydrogen molecule.

subject: Chemistry
question_text: For oxidation of iron, {tex}4 \mathrm{Fe}(\mathrm{s})+3 \mathrm{O}_{2}(\mathrm{g}) \rightarrow 2 \mathrm{Fe}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{3}(\mathrm{s}){/tex} entropy change is – 549.4 JK –1 mol –1 at 298 K. Inspite of negative entropy change of this reaction, why is the reaction spontaneous? ( {tex}\Delta_{\Gamma} H^{\ominus}{/tex} for this reaction is –1648 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 3 J mol –1 )
question_html:
For oxidation of iron,
{tex}4 \mathrm{Fe}(\mathrm{s})+3 \mathrm{O}_{2}(\mathrm{g}) \rightarrow 2 \mathrm{Fe}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{3}(\mathrm{s}){/tex}
entropy change is – 549.4 JK–1 mol–1 at 298 K. Inspite of negative entropy change of this reaction, why is the reaction spontaneous?
({tex}\Delta_{\Gamma} H^{\ominus}{/tex} for this reaction is –1648 {tex}\times{/tex} 103 J mol–1)
solution_text: One decides the spontaneity of a reaction by considering {tex}\Delta S_{\text {total}}\left(\Delta S_{\text {sys }}+\Delta S_{\text {surr }}\right){/tex} . For calculating {tex}\Delta{/tex} S surr , we have to consider the heat absorbed by the surroundings which is equal to {tex}-\Delta_{r} H^{\ominus}{/tex} . At temperature T, entropy change of the surroundings is {tex}\Delta S_{\text {surr }}{/tex} = {tex}-\frac{\Delta_{r} H^{\ominus}}{T}{/tex} (at constant pressure) = {tex}-\frac{\left(-1648 \times 10^{3} \mathrm{Jmol}^{-1}\right)}{298 \mathrm{K}}{/tex} = 5530 JK –1 mol –1 Thus, total entropy change for this reaction {tex}\Delta{/tex} r S total = 5530 JK -1 mol -1 + (-549.4 JK -1 mol -1 ) = 4980.6 JK –1 mol –1 This shows that the above reaction is spontaneous.
solution_html:
One decides the spontaneity of a reaction by considering
{tex}\Delta S_{\text {total}}\left(\Delta S_{\text {sys }}+\Delta S_{\text {surr }}\right){/tex}. For calculating {tex}\Delta{/tex}Ssurr, we have to consider the heat absorbed by the surroundings which is equal to {tex}-\Delta_{r} H^{\ominus}{/tex}. At temperature T, entropy change of the surroundings is {tex}\Delta S_{\text {surr }}{/tex}= {tex}-\frac{\Delta_{r} H^{\ominus}}{T}{/tex} (at constant pressure)
= {tex}-\frac{\left(-1648 \times 10^{3} \mathrm{Jmol}^{-1}\right)}{298 \mathrm{K}}{/tex}
= 5530 JK–1mol–1
Thus, total entropy change for this reaction
{tex}\Delta{/tex}rStotal = 5530 JK-1  mol-1 + (-549.4 JK-1 mol-1)
= 4980.6 JK–1 mol–1
This shows that the above reaction is spontaneous.

question_text: Calculate {tex}\Delta _ { r } \mathrm { G } ^ { \circ }{/tex} for conversion of oxygen to ozone, {tex}{ \frac 3 2 } \mathrm { O } _ { 2 } ( g ) \longrightarrow \mathrm { O } _ { 3 } ( g ) {/tex} at 298 K. if K p for this conversion is 2.47 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -29 .

solution_text: Reaction: {tex}{ \frac 3 2 } \mathrm { O } _ { 2 } ( g ) \rightarrow \mathrm { O } _ { 3 } ( g ) {/tex} Given, K p = 2.47 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -29 and T = 298 K. solution_html:
Reaction:
{tex}{ \frac 3 2 } \mathrm { O } _ { 2 } ( g ) \rightarrow \mathrm { O } _ { 3 } ( g ) {/tex} 
Given, Kp = 2.47 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-29 and T = 298 K.

{tex}\Rightarrow {/tex} {tex}\Delta _ { r } \mathrm { G } ^ { \circ }{/tex} = {tex} - 2.303 \times 8.314\times 298\text{ log}\ (2.47  \times 10^{-29}){/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow {/tex} {tex}\Delta _ { r } \mathrm { G } ^ { \circ } {/tex} = 163229 J mol-1

question_text: At 60 o C, dinitrogen tetroxide is fifty percent dissociated. Calculate the standard free energy change at this temperature and at one atmosphere.
question_html: At 60o C, dinitrogen tetroxide is fifty percent dissociated. Calculate the standard free energy change at this temperature and at one atmosphere.
solution_text: According to the question, at 60 o C, dinitrogen tetroxide is fifty percent dissociated. Reaction: {tex}\mathrm { N } _ { 2 } \mathrm { O } _ { 4 } ( g ) \rightleftharpoons 2 \mathrm { NO } _ { 2 } ( g ){/tex} If N 2 O 4 is 50 % dissociated, {tex}x _ { \mathrm { N } _ { 2 } \mathrm { O } _ { 4 } } = \frac { 1 - 0.5 } { 1 + 0.5 }{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow x _ { \mathrm { NO } _ { 2 } } = \frac { 2 \times 0.5 } { 1 + 0.5 }{/tex} {tex}p _ { \mathrm { N } _ { 2 } \mathrm { O } _ { 4 } } = \frac { 0.5 } { 1.5 } \times 1\; \mathrm { atm } , \ p _ { \mathrm { NO } _ { 2 } } = \frac { 1 } { 1.5 } \times 1 \;\mathrm { atm }{/tex} The equilibrium constant K p is given by {tex}K _ { p } = \frac { \left( p _ { \mathrm { NO } _ { 2 } } \right) ^ { 2 } } { p _ { \mathrm { N } _ { 2 } \mathrm { O } _ { 4 } } } = \frac { 1.5 } { ( 1.5 ) ^ { 2 } ( 0.5 ) } = 1.33 \;\mathrm { atm }{/tex} Since {tex}\Delta{/tex} r G o = –RT ln K p {tex}\Delta{/tex} r G o = -8.314 JK -1 mol -1 {tex}\times{/tex} 333 K {tex}\times{/tex} 2.303 {tex}\times{/tex} 0.1239 = -763.8 kJ mol -1
solution_html:
According to the question, at 60o C, dinitrogen tetroxide is fifty percent dissociated.
Reaction: 
{tex}\mathrm { N } _ { 2 } \mathrm { O } _ { 4 } ( g ) \rightleftharpoons 2 \mathrm { NO } _ { 2 } ( g ){/tex}
If N2O4 is 50 % dissociated,
{tex}x _ { \mathrm { N } _ { 2 } \mathrm { O } _ { 4 } } = \frac { 1 - 0.5 } { 1 + 0.5 }{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow x _ { \mathrm { NO } _ { 2 } } = \frac { 2 \times 0.5 } { 1 + 0.5 }{/tex}
{tex}p _ { \mathrm { N } _ { 2 } \mathrm { O } _ { 4 } } = \frac { 0.5 } { 1.5 } \times 1\; \mathrm { atm } , \ p _ { \mathrm { NO } _ { 2 } } = \frac { 1 } { 1.5 } \times 1 \;\mathrm { atm }{/tex}
The equilibrium constant Kp is given by
{tex}K _ { p } = \frac { \left( p _ { \mathrm { NO } _ { 2 } } \right) ^ { 2 } } { p _ { \mathrm { N } _ { 2 } \mathrm { O } _ { 4 } } } = \frac { 1.5 } { ( 1.5 ) ^ { 2 } ( 0.5 ) } = 1.33 \;\mathrm { atm }{/tex}
Since
{tex}\Delta{/tex}rGo = –RT ln Kp
{tex}\Delta{/tex}rGo = -8.314 JK-1 mol-1 {tex}\times{/tex} 333 K {tex}\times{/tex} 2.303 {tex}\times{/tex} 0.1239 = -763.8 kJ mol-1

question_text: In a process, 701 J of heat is absorbed by a system and 394 J of work is done by the system. What is the change in internal energy for the process?
question_html: In a process, 701 J of heat is absorbed by a system and 394 J of work is done by the system. What is the change in internal energy for the process?
solution_text: We know that, {tex}\Delta{/tex} U = {tex}\Delta{/tex} H + W Where {tex}\Delta{/tex} U is changed in internal energy {tex}\Delta{/tex} H changes in enthalpy And W is work done on the system. Given, 701 J of heat is taken by the system Thus {tex}\Delta{/tex} H = 701 J And 394 J of work is done by the system Thus W = - 394 {tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\Delta{/tex} U = 701 – 394 = 307 J
solution_html:
We know that,
{tex}\Delta{/tex}U = {tex}\Delta{/tex}H + W
Where {tex}\Delta{/tex}U is changed in internal energy
{tex}\Delta{/tex} H changes in enthalpy
And W is work done on the system.
Given, 701 J of heat is taken by the system
Thus {tex}\Delta{/tex}H = 701 J
And 394 J of work is done by the system
Thus W = - 394
{tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\Delta{/tex}U = 701 – 394
= 307 J

question_text: The reaction of cyanamide, NH 2 CN (s), with dioxygen was carried out in a bomb calorimeter, and {tex}\triangle{/tex} U was found to be 742.7 kJ mol -1 at 298 K. Calculate enthalpy change for the reaction at 298 K. NH 2 CN(s) + {tex}\frac{3}{2}{/tex} O 2 (g) {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} N 2 (g) + CO 2 (g) + H 2 O(l)
question_html:
The reaction of cyanamide, NH2CN (s), with dioxygen was carried out in a bomb calorimeter, and {tex}\triangle{/tex}U was found to be 742.7 kJ mol-1 at 298 K. Calculate enthalpy change for the reaction at 298 K.
NH2CN(s) + {tex}\frac{3}{2}{/tex}O2(g) {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} N2(g) + CO2(g) + H2O(l)
solution_text: Enthalpy change for a reaction ( {tex}\Delta \mathrm{H}{/tex} ) is given by the expression, {tex}\Delta \mathrm{H}=\Delta \mathrm{U}+\Delta{\mathrm{n_g}} \mathrm{RT}{/tex} Where {tex}\Delta{/tex} U = change in internal energy {tex}\Delta \mathrm{n}_{g}{/tex} = change in the number of moles For the given reaction, {tex}\Delta n_{g}=\Sigma n_{g}(\text { products })-\Sigma n_{g} (reactants){/tex} = (2 - 1.5) moles {tex}\Sigma n_{g}{/tex} =+0.5 moles And, {tex}\Delta \mathrm{U}{/tex} = -742.7 kJmol -1 T = 298 K R = 8.314 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 3 kJmol -1 K -1 Substituting the values in the expression of {tex}\Delta{/tex} H: {tex}\Delta {/tex} H = (-742.7 kJ mol -1 ) + (+0.5 mol)(298 K) 8.314 {tex} \times {/tex} 10 3 kmol 4 K 4 = -742.7 + 12 {tex}\Delta \mathrm{H}{/tex} (enthalpy change) = -741.5 kJmol -1
solution_html:
Enthalpy change for a reaction ( {tex}\Delta \mathrm{H}{/tex} ) is given by the expression,
{tex}\Delta \mathrm{H}=\Delta \mathrm{U}+\Delta{\mathrm{n_g}} \mathrm{RT}{/tex}
Where {tex}\Delta{/tex}U = change in internal energy
{tex}\Delta \mathrm{n}_{g}{/tex}= change in the number of moles
For the given reaction,
{tex}\Delta n_{g}=\Sigma n_{g}(\text { products })-\Sigma n_{g} (reactants){/tex}
= (2 - 1.5) moles
{tex}\Sigma n_{g}{/tex}=+0.5 moles
And, {tex}\Delta \mathrm{U}{/tex} = -742.7 kJmol-1
T = 298 K
R = 8.314 {tex}\times{/tex} 103 kJmol-1 K-1
Substituting the values in the expression of {tex}\Delta{/tex}H:
{tex}\Delta {/tex}H = (-742.7 kJ mol-1) + (+0.5 mol)(298 K) 8.314 {tex} \times {/tex} 103 kmol4K4
= -742.7 + 12
{tex}\Delta \mathrm{H}{/tex} (enthalpy change)  = -741.5 kJmol-1

solution_text: No. of moles of Al (m) = {tex}\frac{(60 \ g)}{\left(27 g \ mol^{-1}\right)}{/tex} = 2.22 mol Molar heat capacity (C) = 24 J mol -1 K -1 Rise in temperature ( {tex}\Delta{/tex} T) = 55 - 35 = 20°C = 20K Heat evolved (q) = C {tex}\times{/tex} m {tex}\times{/tex} T = (24 J mol -1 K -1 ) {tex}\times{/tex} (2.22 mol) {tex}\times{/tex} (20K) = 1065.6 J = 1.067 kJ
solution_html:

Molar heat capacity (C) = 24 J mol-1 K-1

= 1065.6 J = 1.067 kJ

question_text: Calculate the enthalpy change on freezing of 1.0 mol of water at 10.0 o C to ice at -10.0 o C. {tex}\Delta{/tex} fus H = 6.03 kJ mol -1 at 0 o C. C p [H 2 O(1)] = 75.3 Jmol -1 K -1 ; C p [H 2 O(s)] = 36.8 J mol -1 K -1 .
question_html: Calculate the enthalpy change on freezing of 1.0 mol of water at 10.0oC to ice at -10.0oC. {tex}\Delta{/tex}fus H = 6.03 kJ mol-1 at 0oC. Cp [H2O(1)] = 75.3 Jmol-1 K-1 ; Cp[H2O(s)] = 36.8 J mol-1 K-1.
solution_text: The change may be represented as: According to Hess's Law: {tex}\Delta{/tex} H = {tex}\Delta{/tex} H 1 + {tex}\Delta{/tex} H 2 + {tex}\Delta{/tex} H 3 {tex}\Delta{/tex} H 1 = 75.3 J mol -1 K -1 (10 K) = 753 mol -1 {tex}\Delta{/tex} H 2 (solidification) = -6.03 kJ mol -1 = -6030 J mol -1 (sign changed) {tex}\Delta{/tex} H 3 = 36.8 J mol -1 K -1 (-10 K) = - 36.8 J mol -1 {tex}\Delta{/tex} H = (753 - 6030 - 368) J mol -1 = - 5645 J mol -1 = - 5.645 kJ mol -1
solution_html:
The change may be represented as:

According to Hess's Law:
{tex}\Delta{/tex}H = {tex}\Delta{/tex}H1 + {tex}\Delta{/tex}H2 + {tex}\Delta{/tex}H3
{tex}\Delta{/tex}H1 = 75.3 J mol-1 K-1 (10 K) = 753 mol-1
{tex}\Delta{/tex}H2(solidification) = -6.03 kJ mol-1 = -6030 J mol-1
(sign changed)
{tex}\Delta{/tex}H3 = 36.8 J mol-1 K-1 (-10 K) = - 36.8 J mol-1
{tex}\Delta{/tex}H = (753 - 6030 - 368) J mol-1 = - 5645 J mol-1
= - 5.645 kJ mol-1

Reaction: {tex}\mathrm { N } _ { 2 } \mathrm { O } _ { 4 } ( g ) + 3 \mathrm { CO } ( g ) \longrightarrow \mathrm { N } _ { 2 } \mathrm { O } ( g ) + 3 \mathrm { CO } _ { 2 } ( g ){/tex} We know that, Heat of reaction, {tex}\Delta _ { r } H ^ { \circ } = \Sigma \Delta _ { f } H _ { \text { products } } ^ { \circ } - \Sigma \Delta _ { f } H _ { \text { reactants } } ^ { \circ }{/tex} {tex}= \left[ \Delta _ { f } H ^ { \circ } \left( \mathrm { N } _ { 2 } \mathrm { O } \right) + 3 \Delta _ { f } H ^ { \circ } \left( \mathrm { CO } _ { 2 } \right) \right]{/tex} {tex}- \left[ \Delta _ { f } H ^ { \circ } \left( \mathrm { N } _ { 2 } \mathrm { O } _ { 4 } \right) + 3 \Delta _ { f } H ^ { \circ } ( \mathrm { CO } ) \right]{/tex} {tex}= [ 81 + ( 3 \times - 393 ) ] - [ 9.7 + ( 3 \times - 110 ) ] \mathrm { }{/tex} {tex}= - 777.7 \mathrm {\; kJ } \\ \approx - 778 \mathrm { \;kJ }{/tex}

question_text: Given N 2 (g) + 3H 2 (g) {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} 2NH 3 (g); {tex}\Delta_{r} H^{\ominus}{/tex} = -92.4 kJ mol -1 What is the standard enthalpy of formation of NH 3 gas?
question_html: Given N2 (g) + 3H2 (g) {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} 2NH3 (g); {tex}\Delta_{r} H^{\ominus}{/tex} = -92.4 kJ mol-1 What is the standard enthalpy of formation of NH3 gas?
solution_text: The standard enthalpy of formation of a compound is the change in enthalpy that takes place during the formation of 1 mole of a substance in its standard form from its constituent elements in their standard state. Re-writing the given equation for 1 mole of NH 3 g. {tex}\frac{1}{2} \mathrm{N}_{201}+\frac{3}{2} \mathrm{H}_{240} \longrightarrow 2 \mathrm{NH}_{(3)}{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} Standard enthalpy of formation of NH 3 (g) = {tex}{1 \over 2} \Delta_{1} H^{\ominus}{/tex} = {tex}{1 \over 2} \Delta_{1} H^{\ominus}{/tex} = {tex}{1 \over 2}\left(-92.4 \mathrm{kJ} \mathrm{mol}^{-1}\right){/tex} = -46.2 KJ mol -1 Hence, enthalpy of formation = -46.2KJmol-1.
solution_html:
The standard enthalpy of formation of a compound is the change in enthalpy that takes place during the formation of 1 mole of a substance in its standard form from its constituent elements in their standard state. Re-writing the given equation for 1 mole of NH3 g.
{tex}\frac{1}{2} \mathrm{N}_{201}+\frac{3}{2} \mathrm{H}_{240} \longrightarrow 2 \mathrm{NH}_{(3)}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Standard enthalpy of formation of NH3(g) = {tex}{1 \over 2} \Delta_{1} H^{\ominus}{/tex}
= {tex}{1 \over 2} \Delta_{1} H^{\ominus}{/tex}
= {tex}{1 \over 2}\left(-92.4 \mathrm{kJ} \mathrm{mol}^{-1}\right){/tex}
= -46.2 KJ mol-1
Hence, enthalpy of formation = -46.2KJmol-1.

question_text: Calculate the enthalpy change for the process CCl 4 (g) {tex}\longrightarrow{/tex} C(g) + 4Cl(g) and calculate the bond enthalpy of C - CI in CCI 4 (g) {tex}\Delta _ { \mathrm { vap } } H ^ \ominus{/tex} (CCl 4 ) = 30.5 kJ mol -1 {tex}\Delta _ {f} H ^ \ominus{/tex} (CCl 4 ) = -135.5 kJ mol -1 {tex}\Delta _ {a} H ^ \ominus{/tex} (C) = 715.0 kJ mol -1 where, {tex}\Delta _ {a} H ^ \ominus{/tex} is enthalpy is atomisation, {tex}\Delta _ { a } H ^ { \ominus} \left( \mathrm { Cl } _ { 2 } \right){/tex} = 242 kJ mol -1 .
question_html:
Calculate the enthalpy change for the process CCl4 (g) {tex}\longrightarrow{/tex} C(g) + 4Cl(g) and calculate the bond enthalpy of C - CI in CCI4 (g)
{tex}\Delta _ { \mathrm { vap } } H ^ \ominus{/tex} (CCl4) = 30.5 kJ mol-1
{tex}\Delta _ {f} H ^ \ominus{/tex} (CCl4) = -135.5 kJ mol-1
{tex}\Delta _ {a} H ^ \ominus{/tex} (C) = 715.0 kJ mol-1
where, {tex}\Delta _ {a} H ^ \ominus{/tex} is enthalpy is atomisation,
{tex}\Delta _ { a } H ^ { \ominus} \left( \mathrm { Cl } _ { 2 } \right){/tex} = 242 kJ mol-1.
solution_text: According to the question, {tex}\Delta _ { \mathrm { vap } } H ^ { \ominus}{/tex} (CCl 4 ) = 30.5 kJmol -1 , {tex}\Delta _ { f } H ^ { \ominus} {/tex} (CCl 4 ) = - 135.5 kJmol -1 , {tex}\Delta _ { a } H ^ { \ominus }{/tex} (C) = 715.0 kJ mol -1 , {tex}\Delta _ { a } H ^ { \ominus }{/tex} (Cl 2 ) = 242 kJmol -1 . CCl 4 (l) {tex}\longrightarrow{/tex} CCl 4 (g) ; {tex}\Delta _ { \mathrm { vap } } H ^ { \ominus}{/tex} = + 30.5 kJ mol -1 ....(i) C(s) + 2Cl 2 (g) {tex}\longrightarrow{/tex} CCl4(l); {tex}\Delta _ { f } H ^ { \ominus}{/tex} = - 135.5 kJ mol -1 ....(ii) C(s) {tex}\longrightarrow{/tex} C(g); {tex}\Delta _ { a } H ^ { \ominus}{/tex} = 715.0 kJ mol -1 ....(iii) Cl 2 (g) {tex}\longrightarrow{/tex} 2 Cl(g); {tex}\Delta _ { a } H ^ { \ominus}{/tex} = 242 kJ mol -1 ....(iv) Multipling Eq. (iv) by 2, we get​​​​​​​ 2Cl 2 (g) {tex}\longrightarrow{/tex} 4Cl(g); {tex}\Delta _ { a } H ^ { \ominus}{/tex} = 484.0 kJ mol -1 .....(v) Adding Eqs. (iii) and (v), we get​​​​​​​ C(s) + 2Cl 2 (g) {tex}\longrightarrow{/tex} C(g) + 4Cl(g); {tex}\Delta H{/tex} = 1199 kJ mol -1 ....(vi) Reversing Eqs. (i) and (ii), we get​​​​​​​ CCl 4 (g) {tex}\longrightarrow{/tex} CCl 4 (l) ; {tex}\Delta H{/tex} = - 30.5 kJ mol -1 ​​​​​​​....(vii) CCl 4 (l) {tex}\longrightarrow{/tex} C(s) + 2Cl 2 (g) ; {tex}\Delta H {/tex} = + 135.5 kJ mol -1 ....(viii) Adding Eqs. (vi), (vii) and (viii), we get CCl 4 (g) {tex}\longrightarrow{/tex} C(g) + 4Cl(g) ; {tex}\Delta H{/tex} = 1304 kJ mol -1 Now, Bond enthalpy of C-CI bond is CCl 4 = {tex}\frac { 1304 } { 4 }{/tex} = 326 kJ mol -1 .
solution_html:
According to the question, {tex}\Delta _ { \mathrm { vap } } H ^ { \ominus}{/tex} (CCl4) = 30.5 kJmol-1, {tex}\Delta _ { f } H ^ { \ominus} {/tex} (CCl4) = - 135.5 kJmol-1, {tex}\Delta _ { a } H ^ { \ominus }{/tex} (C) = 715.0 kJ mol-1, {tex}\Delta _ { a } H ^ { \ominus }{/tex}(Cl2) = 242 kJmol-1.
CCl4 (l) {tex}\longrightarrow{/tex} CCl4 (g) ; {tex}\Delta _ { \mathrm { vap } } H ^ { \ominus}{/tex}= + 30.5 kJ mol-1....(i)
C(s) + 2Cl2(g) {tex}\longrightarrow{/tex} CCl4(l); {tex}\Delta _ { f } H ^ { \ominus}{/tex} = - 135.5 kJ mol-1....(ii)
C(s) {tex}\longrightarrow{/tex} C(g); {tex}\Delta _ { a } H ^ { \ominus}{/tex} = 715.0 kJ mol-1....(iii)
Cl2 (g) {tex}\longrightarrow{/tex} 2 Cl(g); {tex}\Delta _ { a } H ^ { \ominus}{/tex} = 242 kJ mol-1....(iv)
Multipling Eq. (iv) by 2, we get​​​​​​​
2Cl2(g) {tex}\longrightarrow{/tex} 4Cl(g); {tex}\Delta _ { a } H ^ { \ominus}{/tex} = 484.0 kJ mol-1.....(v)
Adding Eqs. (iii) and (v), we get​​​​​​​
C(s) + 2Cl2(g) {tex}\longrightarrow{/tex} C(g) + 4Cl(g); {tex}\Delta H{/tex} = 1199 kJ mol-1....(vi)
Reversing Eqs. (i) and (ii), we get​​​​​​​
CCl4(g) {tex}\longrightarrow{/tex} CCl4(l) ; {tex}\Delta H{/tex} = - 30.5 kJ mol-1​​​​​​​....(vii)
CCl4(l) {tex}\longrightarrow{/tex} C(s) + 2Cl2(g) ; {tex}\Delta H {/tex} = + 135.5 kJ mol-1....(viii)
Adding Eqs. (vi), (vii) and (viii), we get
CCl4(g) {tex}\longrightarrow{/tex} C(g) + 4Cl(g) ; {tex}\Delta H{/tex} = 1304 kJ mol-1
Now, Bond enthalpy of C-CI bond is CCl4 = {tex}\frac { 1304 } { 4 }{/tex} = 326 kJ mol-1.

question_text: For an isolated system, {tex}\Delta{/tex} U = 0, what will be {tex}\Delta{/tex} S?
question_html: For an isolated system, {tex}\Delta{/tex}U = 0, what will be {tex}\Delta{/tex}S?
solution_text: Change in internal energy ( {tex}\Delta{/tex} U) for an isolated system is zero for it does not exchange any energy with the surroundings. But entropy tends to increase in case of spontaneous reaction. Therefore, {tex}\Delta{/tex} S > 0 or positive.

question_html: For the reaction, 2A(g) + B(g) {tex}\longrightarrow{/tex} 2D (g); {tex}\Delta U ^o{/tex} = -10.5 kJ and {tex}\Delta S ^o{/tex} = -44.1 JK-1. solution_text: According to the question, {tex}\Delta U ^o{/tex} = - 10.5 kJ and {tex}\Delta S ^o{/tex} = - 44.1 JK -1 , R = 8.314 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -3 kJ mol -1 , T = 298 K. Reaction: 2A(g) + B(g) {tex}\longrightarrow{/tex} 2D (g) {tex}\Delta{/tex} n g = n p - n r = 2 - 3 = -1 We know that, {tex}\Delta H ^o = \Delta U ^o + \Delta n _ { g } R T{/tex} {tex}\Delta H ^o{/tex} = - 10.5 + (- 1 {tex}\times{/tex} 8.314 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -3 {tex}\times{/tex} 298) = - 12.977 kJ mol -1 Now, {tex}\Delta G ^o = \Delta H ^o - T \Delta S ^o{/tex} {tex}\Delta G ^o{/tex} = -12.977 - ( 298 {tex}\times{/tex} - 44.1 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -3 ) = 0.165 kJ mol -1 The reaction will not occur spontaneously because {tex}\Delta G ^o{/tex} is positive.
solution_html:
According to the question, {tex}\Delta U ^o{/tex} = - 10.5 kJ and {tex}\Delta S ^o{/tex} = - 44.1 JK-1, R = 8.314 {tex}\times{/tex}  10-3 kJ mol-1, T = 298 K.
Reaction:
2A(g) + B(g) {tex}\longrightarrow{/tex} 2D (g)
{tex}\Delta{/tex}ng = np - nr = 2 - 3 = -1
We know that, {tex}\Delta H ^o = \Delta U ^o + \Delta n _ { g } R T{/tex}
{tex}\Delta H ^o{/tex} = - 10.5 + (- 1 {tex}\times{/tex} 8.314 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-3 {tex}\times{/tex} 298)
= - 12.977 kJ mol-1
Now, {tex}\Delta G ^o = \Delta H ^o - T \Delta S ^o{/tex}
{tex}\Delta G ^o{/tex}  = -12.977 - ( 298 {tex}\times{/tex} - 44.1 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-3)

question_text: Comment on the thermodynamic stability of NO(g), given {tex}{\frac 12} N_2 (g) + {\frac 12} O_2 (g) → NO(g) ; ∆_rH^{\ominus} {/tex} = 90 kJ mol -1 {tex}NO(g) + {\frac 12} O_2 (g) → NO_2 (g) : \Delta_rH^\ominus{/tex} = -74 kJ mol -1
question_html:
Comment on the thermodynamic stability of NO(g), given
{tex}{\frac 12} N_2 (g) + {\frac 12} O_2 (g) → NO(g) ; ∆_rH^{\ominus} {/tex} = 90 kJ mol-1
{tex}NO(g) + {\frac 12} O_2 (g) → NO_2 (g) : \Delta_rH^\ominus{/tex} = -74 kJ mol-1
solution_text: The positive value of {tex}\Delta_{n} \mathrm{H}{/tex} indicates that heat is absorbed during the formation of NO(g). This means that NO(g) has higher energy than the reactants (N 2 and O 2 ) Hence, NO(g) is unstable. The negative value of {tex}\Delta rH{/tex} indicates that heat is evolved during the formation of NO 2 (g) from NO(g) and O 2 (g). The product, NO 2 (g) is stabilized with minimum energy. Hence, NO(g) is unstable.

So, q surr = + 286 kJ mol -1 We know that, {tex}\Delta S = \frac { q _ { \text { surr } } } { T }{/tex} {tex}= \frac { + 286 \mathrm { kJ } \mathrm { mol } ^ { - 1 } } { 298 \mathrm { K } }{/tex} = 0.9597 kJ K -1 mol -1 = 959.9 JK -1 mol -1 .
